topic: Numeric - From and TryFrom Between Integer Types

turns.content: I have a function that converts u8 to i32 using from, but I'm worried about overflow when the byte is greater than 127. Is there a better way to handle this safely?

You are right to be concerned because From is lossless and will panic on invalid data instead of returning an error. For narrowing conversions like u8 to i32, you should use TryFrom which returns a Result<_, TryFromIntError> so your code can handle the failure gracefully.
That sounds better but I still don't understand why i32::try_from(u64) is different from using TryFrom on a trait. Can you explain more about the difference between these two patterns?

The distinction matters because try_from_trait requires an explicit receiver and may not work directly on primitive integers without extension traits, whereas i32::try_from(u64) is a built-in method that handles the conversion logic internally for specific integer types in std.
I think you are wrong about that. I found that u8 can be converted to i32 using try_from_trait with TryFromIntError, so why would I ever use the method syntax? Is there a performance difference?

There is no significant performance difference between these two approaches because both ultimately call into the same underlying conversion logic in std. However, i32::try_from(u64) is more idiomatic for direct primitive conversions while try_from_trait is necessary when you need to convert a custom type that implements TryFrom.
